With certain exceptions, any question received by midnight Monday Eastern time will get an answer. Please note:  Any question received after then will not get an answer, and in fact will just be deleted. I've been getting an increasing number of people trying to post after these are closed, so will have to draw a harder line than before.) If you're in a hurry, or suspect you may be the 143,916th person to ask a question, please check out the very rough version 1.0 of The Magic Monday FAQ here. Also: I will not be putting through or answering any more questions about practicing magic around children. I've answered those in simple declarative sentences in the FAQ. If you read the FAQ and don't think your question has been answered, read it again. The image? I've decided to trace, as far as I can, my own occult lineage in photos. We're still tracing Juliet Ashley's end of the lineage. Three weeks ago I posted an image of her fourth teacher, Arthur Edward Waite, the Golden Dawn alumnus who passed onto her the rituals that became the foundation for the Fellowship of the Hermetic Rose. Waite, in turn, got his knowledge from the founders of the Hermetic Order of the Golden Dawn. Over the last two weeks I posted two of those founders, and here's the third, usually neglected figure in that lineage, Moina Mathers. She was the sister of the French philosopher Henri Bergson, author of Creative Evolution. A capable occultist as well as a talented artist, she wads the first person initiated into the Golden Dawn, became one of its leading members, married Samuel Mathers, and remained the head of one of the surviving branches of the Order until her death in 1928. She was an extremely influential figure in early 20th century occultism, and several other lines we'll be tracing lead back to her.

I don't have a question, but I have two updates on things from prior Magic Mondays:

a) I was also the person who speculated about the possibility that new planets change the laws of physics on the material plane, and have collected a decent amount of evidence to suggest the possibility needs to be considered. One of the core reasons for stating this is the number of biological theories which emerged in the era when Ceres was a planet, and were quickly discredited afterwards. The usual narrative is that these were efforts to explain the world based on incomplete and inaccurate data that was subsequently determined to be false, but the primary evidence from the era suggests the possibility that the scientists, such as Lamarck, were not wrong, but observing a natural world that worked differently. Given the focus of Ceres on wilderness, it makes abundant sense that much of her effect would be in the natural world; and biology in particular among the sciences seemed to be describing a different world during the Romantic Era than before or after.

There was also “Romantic Chemistry” and “Romantic Medicine”, both of which shared the same temporal limits with the broader Romantic Movement, and both of which included claims of techniques and results which were, based on the thought of both before and after the era, as well as experiments and research done later, impossible.

Additionally, looking into the luminiferous aether reveals a weird pattern, whereby there was always some evidence for it, but it was never conclusive; there were always experiments which seemed to provide evidence for it, always others which seemed to refute it, and others which were inconclusive. Given that this is the exact pattern with Vulcan, it seems to suggest a link, and provide further evidence for the natural world changing with planets.

Finally, there's also the radical shifts in how the world was viewed when Uranus and Neptune were discovered, but determining whether or not these were shifts in how we saw the world, or the world itself is a challenge, while the evidence that some natural laws changed during the eras when Ceres was a planet, and when Vulcan was thought by some to exist provides a way to test these possibilities.

(Yes, I'm currently in the process of more research, and writing a book on this topic)

b) A while back, you suggested that metal music is Uranian, in response to someone else asking about certain Plutonian aspects of the genre. It's hit me, as a metal fan, that metal is both Uranian and Plutonian. Metal was born in the late 1960s, and until 2006 displayed all the hallmarks of a Plutonian Phenomena: an obsession with sex and violence; a division between the performances and recordings (such that very often they sound like different songs); an obsession with abstract work and a disregard for what worked; and the whole nine yards.

This started to change shortly after 2006, and now I think you're right metal is a Uranian phenomena, but I think it started out as a Plutonian one, and that a sizable fraction of the metal music from before it made this shift will be utterly incomprehensible to people in the near future. In fact, I think we can already see this happening, as a lot of people who enjoy the genre now look back at earlier metal music and simply cannot understand what they once saw in it; or people with strong Uranian influences, such as myself, get drawn to a genre that we once could not stand as it shifts towards a new planetary influence.

comments.title: Hermitix on Steiner and Weber, Celtic-Germanic Myth Comparisons
comments.content:
Good Evening,

I hope everyone's week is off to a good start, and I offer my best wishes and blessings to anyone who would have them. I also offer my thanks to our host for this space and to everyone whose participation makes these weekly posts so interesting and lively.

To Share: After JMG's recent return to the Hermitix podcast (https://www.patreon.com/posts/druidry-with-73517125), I've been poking around the back catalog and rather enjoying it. For example, I've just wrapped up listening to every episode in the archive that focuses on Rudolf Steiner, and there was a two-parter with a modern Oswald Spengler scholar, I've listend to a couple on Nietzsche, and now I'm delving into those episodes that cover Jung. Of these, the one I'd draw the most attention to is "Steiner, Weber, and the Disenchantment of Modernity with Aaron French" (https://www.patreon.com/posts/steiner-weber-of-63883938), which argues that Steiner and Weber had more in common in how they viewed the "disenchantment of the world" (as referenced in the introduction to the Druidry Handbook, of course) than is commonly supposed, but that each had rather different solutions. This one's interesting for being an interview with a religious studies scholar of esoteric traditions who seems a bit skeptical about the reality of esoteric beliefs, but at least seems to take them seriously within the worldview of the folks he's writing about/discussing.

To Ask: For JMG or anyone else with thoughts: any recommendations of sources that specifically compare Celtic and Germanic mythology (as opposed to broader Indo-European comparative mythology that draws on both of those traditions along with all the others)? I've been using Will Parker's The Four Branches of the Mabinogi as one of the translations/commentaries I'm drawing on to meditate through the Mabinogion for the Dolmen Arch, and time and again I've been struck by elements with remarkable similarity between the Celtic antecedents to the Mabinogion and what I know from the Eddas and other Germanic sources. I think this would be an especially fruitful source of meditation themes for me, and any help on where to go (besides "check out the bibliography of Parker's Book!") would be much appreciated.

Thanks very much,
Jeff

replies.title: Re: Hermitix on Steiner and Weber, Celtic-Germanic Myth Comparisons
replies.content: It's not primarily about myth, but Michael J. Enright's Lady With A Mead Cup explores the extraordinary common ground between warband culture in Celtic and Germanic peoples, and touches on a lot of information relevant to your interest.

replies.title: Re: Hermitix on Steiner and Weber, Celtic-Germanic Myth Comparisons
replies.content:
Ah, wonderful, thank you! I did an Adler-style "inspectional reading" of Lady With a Mead Cup late last year with the intent of reading in more depth soon after, but then I hit on my resolution to read only books by dead authors for the year. I'll have to make sure this is near the top of the list for 2023.

(For anyone following along at home scratching your head at Parker's book: since I am not reading The Four Branches of the Mabinogi cover-to-cover and am using it directly for meditation, I'm treating it as falling under the category of "[for] a craft taken on soon after.")

replies.content:
Take a look at Maria Kvilhaug's short book The Maiden with the Mead: A Goddess of Initiation in Norse Mythology (2004), whichthe author has made available for free online at:

www.duo.uio.no/bitstream/handle/10852/23958/18497.pdf?sequence=1&isAllowed=y

She cites Enright's work in it.

Her much larger work on Germanic mythology, The Seed of Yggdrasil, can be purchased in digital form for a modest sum at:

replies.title: Re: Hermitix on Steiner and Weber, Celtic-Germanic Myth Comparisons
replies.content:
With the caveat that I haven't read them yet, a couple items from my to-read-someday list appear to hit the mark:

Myths and Symbols in Pagan Europe: Early Scandinavian and Celtic Religions by H.R. Ellis Davidson
This book directly compares Celtic, germanic, and nordic myths and cultures.

Nordic Religions in the Viking Age by Thomas A. DuBois
This book focuses more on the Nordic culture, but it delves much deeper into it's context - the interactions with other cultures and traditions, and the historical trajectories that led to and developed from the Nordic peoples.

Wishing you and your family well.

And the DuBois work has also been on my "to get to" list, but I didn't realize it's emphasis on interaction with outside cultures - sounds very helpful.

replies.title: Re: Hermitix on Steiner and Weber, Celtic-Germanic Myth Comparisons
replies.content:
Maybe Myths and symbols in pagan Europe. Early Scandinavian and Celtic religions by H. R. Ellis Davidson (1988, Manchester University Press) might be worth a look, if you're not aware of it already.

From the back cover: "The Celts and Germans and Scandinavians had much in common in their religious practices and beliefs and this is the first serious attempt that has been made to compare them. There are striking resemblances in their ideas about battle-goddesses and protective spirits, holy places, sacrificial rituals, divination and ideas about the Other World, and Myths and symbols in pagan Europe poses questions like: do such parallels go back to early times or are they due to late Viking contact?"

All the best,
Owain D.

comments.title: Prayer request
comments.content:
Hello all,

I am a regular reader/commenter and have been for a couple of years now (thank you JMG!)

I was hoping I could request prayers from the Magic Monday community.

I am currently pregnant and mid-way through the third trimester. I have planned on birthing at home under the care of a midwife-only birth center. However, they are constrained by many rules and regulations and will need to transfer my care to the local hospital if my baby doesn't turn into an 'optimal' birthing position in the next few weeks (head down). If I am transferred to the local hospital I will most likely be made to have a c-section and will be subject to a whole host of dangerous medical interventions. I suffered many years of medical trauma and nothing scares me more. It is well-known among midwives that the interventions cause more damage and trauma in the modern medical system than anything else.

I'm hoping for prayers to watch over my bub and I and for a healthy natural birth without intervention from the medical system.

Thank you in advance.

replies.title: Re: Prayer request
replies.content:
My prayers are with you and your baby.

I went into labor 10 days before the due date,and then Sonkitten got stuck, so I ended up having a Caesarean. My theory is he was eager to get out into the world, then got close enough to the exit to see what all goes on out here, and tried to reverse course and get safely back inside. 😊. I mention it because we were both fine, and that was 43 years ago. Midwives and natural births were just beginning to catch on; the average person had access to neither. I had the “classical” vertical incision rather then the “bikini “ horizontal incision, because with the classical incision there’s a good possibility subsequent births can be non-surgical. My scar was about 8 inches long, though after 43 years it’s no longer visible. Sonkitten was born around 9 p.m. and the next day I was walking down the hall to the nursery. When you stand up the first time, a lot of blood will fall out; this is startling but normal, don’t let it bother you. There will be some bleeding for several days, nothing that kotexes can’t handle. Do NOT use tampons until you get the medical okay, as they can introduce infection. You’ll want help putting your shoes on for about a week. Good luck, and enjoy your new family member!

—Princess Cutekitten

replies.title: Re: Prayer request
replies.content:
P.S. I forgot to mention, one of my roommates was on her EIGHTH (!) Caesarean, and doing fine. 43 years ago.

—Princess Cutekitten

replies.title: Re: Prayer request
replies.content: Prayers for an all-natural and healthy birth, on the way!

replies.title: Re: Prayer request
replies.content:
Blessings and a hug (if you can stand one right now) are on the way.

There are also things which can be tried to make the baby change position. I know about moxa stimulation (by a competent midwife or naturopath who knows what they are doing and is closely monitoring the baby), for example.

There is also the option of trying to „turn“ the baby in the womb by hand, by pushing in the right direction. This needs to be very closely supervised in case the umbilical cord gets stuck around the baby‘s neck, in which case an immediate caesarean would be necessary. (That‘s also why it‘s only tried if the baby hasn‘t turned at a very late stage, because you don‘t want to risk the caesarean before that time). As far as I know, though, an experienced „turner“ has quite a good success rate and the number of caesareans is low, so maybe that would be an option for you. There may be other options I‘m not aware of, too.

I‘m not a medical professional, and in any case, you will need a competent professional to perform any of these (I‘m fairly cool with home treatments, but the external, manual turning in particular is NOT treat-at-home!!)

Finally, I‘m not sure about the situation in your area of the world, but breech deliveries (is that the right term?) can and are done successfully in lots of hospitals. Maybe there are other hospitals in your area which would be willing to try a natural birth and only resort to a caesarean if absolutely necessary?

What I‘m trying to say is: I‘m not sure if you have looked into options, and which options are available to you at all, but maybe there is something that could be done. :-)

And for what it‘s worth, my midwife used to explain that some kids don‘t turn because they are smart and know that the umbilical cord would wrap around their necks and hurt them during birth if they tried - so if all should fail, maybe this is your very smart baby doing what‘s best in the long term. On the other hand, some kids turn all by themselves at a very late stage, so maybe yours is just taking a bit longer to get sorted. :-)

Milkyway

replies.title: Re: Prayer request
replies.content:
I'll pray for you and yours.

I have a similar story for you but I ramble, so first let me say that rebozo and walking a lot, as much as you can, are two natural ways reputed by kooky non-MD's to coax the baby down (standard disclaimer of not medical advice, not endorsed by JMG in any way shape or form, etc.).

If you end up in the hospital they might try to get you to stay in bed to monitor you for your safety and not for their liability or anything like that. Less qualified people might make the flimsy argument that this would keep the baby in a non-optimal position if it still needs to move around into place. Those delusional scoundrels would probably say that you should stay up and walk around instead, but that's just foolish.

After the birth, liver and onions and nettle tea are reputed by non-medical non-professionals as great for restoring blood. Soaking the liver in milk before cooking it takes the edge off of it, this is just culinary advice so I encourage you to follow it. More culinary advice, fry the onions on low heat so they caramelize then turn up the heat for the liver so you get a nice sear. Saving the placenta, too, is spoken of highly by non-MD quacks. If you don't know what's good for you, you can cut off raw pieces and put them into shakes/smoothies and, for longer term use, you can also cut thin slices, run them through a dehydrator, grind that up in a (clean) coffee grinder and put it into capsules. You know, if you are crazy and follow non-medical non-advice from strangers on the internet.

If you catch a c-section, again, some people with no medical knowledge would encourage you to walk as much as you can afterwards. But I would encourage you to talk to your doctor and obey them without question.

Story time... we live in CA, where "my body, my choice" goes out the window when it comes to home births. Our midwife for our first birth screwed us over at the last minute and my wife ended up in the hospital -- my wife always runs late, with kids and with most other things. Legally the midwife couldn't attend to her but she couldn't even be bothered to come to the hospital to help us make informed choices (after promising she would). After several days of induced labor, the doctor basically gave up on us and was all "it's time to meet your baby." So our kid was born via c-section, just in time to have Saturn combust in his natal chart.

I pray the rosary and realized that my wife's whole experience mapped pretty directly onto the sorrowful mysteries. She got all the agony from seeing the screw job coming, being abandoned and knowing she would have to submit to the system. The labor drugs were like a scourging, really nasty stuff. She got all the mocking from nurses and doctors for being a crunchy freak who dared to consider a home birth. When it was time for her c-section, she walked to the OR, wheeling her IV along with her like carrying the cross. When you get the c-section they strap you down crucifixion style. The good news is that the glorious mysteries follow, she got better and we have a healthy boy. Please read that last sentence again, if it all goes south there's still a baby waiting for you afterwards and all the joy and potential for seeing the Holy Spirit in his or her eyes and smile. If you aren't Christian, translate those sentences however you need to, just know that life goes on and babies are really joyful. If you are Christian, also remember that the trials that come from God aren't always easy and aren't always what you expect but that He will give you enough grace to get through them.

Our second kid was born at home, but late and without the midwife present because, you know, CA home birth laws are in place for our safety. My wife bled out a LOT. The midwife could legally come over once the baby was out and was able to give my wife a progesterone shot and multiple bags of saline fluid before it got too bad. We basically called her instead of 911, it was pretty dicey so you might not want to go that route for your first... Our third kid was born at home with a lot less drama and with the midwife present, really easy and a really sweet baby. For the third one we gave our best estimate of when the pregnancy occurred but these things are sometimes hard to gauge.

Again, I'll pray for you and for the child (and let us know if the DAD could use prayers too, it ain't easy). All the best and remember you're not alone.

replies.title: Re: Prayer request
replies.content:
All my love and blessings to you. The hospital trauma is very scary--I know that fear well. Sending you hugs and positive energy, sister.

With the caveat that I'm not a doctor or midwife, just a random lady who's had a lot of kids, and you shouldn't take my advice seriously: everyone's suggestions are good (especially keeping active, crawling around on hands and knees, and moxibustion.) May that work for you, easily and well.

Something else no one has mentioned is simply...not going to the hospital until the baby is almost out. I know a couple ladies who delay and delay and then Oooppsss they show up in the emergency room (or sometimes the front seat of a Honda Civic) and the baby comes out no time for a C-section, oh well, hooray!

If worst comes to worst and you do have to deliver in the hospital, may you be blessed and manage to radiate compassion, calm and humor to the best of your ability. This happened to me once and turned what could have been a very bad situation into a pretty good one.

(Getting cut when you don't want to is really horrible but if you view the doctors, nurses, etc. with compassion, sometimes that raises their level of perception and they start to behave with more compassion and it's a benevolent cycle. May it be so for you if you are forced to go that route.)

My very best wishes to you and your family,

--Ms. Krieger

replies.content:
My wife and I know well the frustration of being stuck in the system. We have two kids, the first was an attempted homebirth that ended in a c-section and the second was a successful vbac that was supposed to happen at a birth center and ended in a totally unnecessary NICU stay.

To be frank, I think we would have been better off with no midwife the first time and we almost certainly would have been better off without the birth center the second time. Just my unqualified opinion, of course.

As Ms. Krieger suggested above, we would definitely wait until labor was WELL under way before going in to any medical care. The less anyone is poking and prodding you while your body is trying to do its thing the better.

We would avoid a children's hospital at ALL costs.

We would try and find a well reputed chiropractor or TCM practitioner to help turn the baby.

When labor started we'd definitely hang out at home for as long as possible, just trying to relax, get sleep and be in a comfortable environment. For my wife, labor has definitely been a marathon and trying to sprint it early was a big part of what led to her c-section.

My wife just chimed in to look at the "spinning babies" website for daily things you can do to help the baby turn.

Best of luck, we'll pray for you

HV

A while back, someone speculated on the possibility that processed food was a Plutonian Phenomena, and you said it seemed plausible, but it's occurred to me that there's a very good reason to think it is: two of the most common additives, high fructose corn syrup (HFCS), and mono-sodium glutamate (MSG), both involve division. The most common sugar in most human diets, sucrose, is composed of two compounds linked together: glucose and fructose. HFCS, meanwhile, is composed of sucrose and fructose, but split apart.

MSG, meanwhile, is a modified form of glutamic acid, a common amino acid. MSG differs from glutamic acid by a single change: the replacement of a hydrogen atom with a sodium ion. For those who don't know technical chemistry terms, an atom in a molecule remains attached, while an ion won't: it will break into two charged particles when exposed to water. (This is why salt, for example, dissolves in water)

So, two of the most common food additives are both artificial and involve division; this is to say nothing of the broader sleaze and corruption of the industry, all of which screams Pluto to me.

replies.content:
My opinion as a parent and teacher of young adults:
Be cautious with doing anything unless you are willing to step in and have a true relationship; and delay making big statements or interventions until you have established a relationship. You are unlikely to redirect years of momentum with a single email.

I've seen all of these versions of failure (in other contexts):
If you suggest magical practice and they take it up while doing psychedelics, it could lead to bigger problems.
If you suggest they stop the psychedelics, they could write you off as a "hater."
If you suggest they stop the drugs and start magical practice and they take you up on it, they likely will expect you to step in as their guide and teacher, and possibly use you and your care as their fuel for change, so that if/when you set boundaries, they drop everything.

Instead of these options, decide your boundaries and goals now. Do you want a relationship with this person? If so, reach out. Connect with them. Be interested in who they are and what they are doing. Tell them stories about their parent when they are ready. Tell them the truth when they ask your opinion, but make sure they know that you caring about them isn't contingent on them doing what you think they should do. Eventually (perhaps after many weeks or months,) you may be at a point where your advice is trusted and maybe both your lives will benefit from the relationship.

replies.title: Re: Doubts about Fellowship Of The Hermetic Rose protection exercises
replies.content:
Thanks for asking -- that's an important question. If you practice spiritism or follow one of the African Diaspora faiths, I recommend using the Judson exercise for your daily protective working. Abby Judson, who introduced it, was a spiritist medium and teacher, and she taught it to her students because she found that it improved their ability to work with their spirit guides. There's a description of the Judson exercise in Volume 1 of the FHR work, but you can find out more about it and download the original sources here.

The Sphere of Protection is also an option; my teacher John Gilbert, who created the current version of that ritual, worked with spirit guides as part of his practices. I don't recommend the Lesser Ritual of the Pentagram for spiritists, since some spirits have difficulty with it, but the other two should be fine.

The effect of a ritual like the Judson exercise is to cleanse your aura of negative energies so that hostile forces and spirits can't affect you. It doesn't repel any spirit with whom you have a positive relationship.

comments.title: Animal posession
comments.content:
Hi JMG,

Are demons able to posess non-human animals? I know you've said how our ability to contact the demonic realm only comes from mistakes made by the human race, possibly in Lemuria many millenia ago, but two things stand out from this:

1) if we reincarnate from animal bodies to human ones, then back again, do demons retain the ability to contact and affect us?

2) In the Bible, the demon(s) Legion is driven out into a herd of swine, who then drown themselves. There are other instances in holy books and general folk tales of animals becoming possessed as well.

The reason I thought of this was because I was listening to a podcast where someone's animals appeared to become possessed by a demonic entity residing on a farm. The pigs were the first, and worst affected, becoming seemingly consumed with a bloodthirsty fury overnight. After the animals were put down, toxicological and microbiological causes were ruled out.

Humans generally seem to be alarmingly similar to pigs in many ways (such as the fact we can accept their organs and blood as transplants, human flesh is supposed to taste like pork and we can get many more diseases from them than other cloven-hooved animals) The fact that several cultures explicitly prohibit pork consumption, and by proxy, keeping pigs (as pigs have little value aside from their meat), makes me think that there is some uncomfortable connection between humans and swine back in our genetic heritage, more so than just the practical measures of preventing animal-borne disease. (And therefore, eating pork is effectively a form of cannibalism)

A friend who worked on a pig farm said that pigs have disturbingly 'human' eyes, and apparently bear grudges and have a far more spiteful nature than all the other farm animals he's worked with, though I did comment to him that maybe that's just because they are intelligent enough to hate being on farms more than other farm animals...

Mr. Crow

replies.title: Re: Rainbows and unicorns

Neither rainbows nor unicorns have any important role in the occult traditions known to me. Unicorns do show up from time to time in alchemical emblems, but then just about everything shows up in alchemical emblems now and then. Rainbows sometimes get some use as a symbol of hope -- the reference is to the end of the Noah's ark story.

I'd suggest interpreting this the way you'd interpret a scrying or a divination. comments.title: Cold water and slo-mo Judson?
comments.content:
Anecdotal data point - Been experimenting with switching from warm mixed stream shower and turning off the hot tap completely (in one go) and then slowly rotating clockwise under the cold water stream.
Rotating slowly for two to three mins or so.

Did this for about 3 weeks and then noticed a slight pushback in terms of 'just skip that today' - a thought idea that seemed to have no origin (since I had been enjoying the experiment).

Thinking back on what I had been doing, this 'push-back' occurred once I started planning to include the head rather than just neck and body in the cold water.

This morning an interesting experience - warm shower about 5 mins, then as I was about to turn off the hot water, the idea of 'skipping that' surfaced. Paused a moment to try and figure what was going on, then decided 'No. Do it.'

Rotated slowly clockwise for about 60 secs and then thought to get my entire head under the shower and, once again, unexpected thought 'this is fine, you don't need to do that' is the closest I can put it.

A clear decision/act of will was required (i.e who is running this show [a la Invictus]?!)

"Do it!"

And I did - now this is the strange bit - standing with head under shower and rotating clockwise slowly I tried something I haven’t tried before – a strong visualisation of body and energy system being washed clean, the water carrying unwanted influence away. Suddenly I had a strong feeling of release and a distinct feeling of something being washed away with tingling down scalp and face like something was dissolving...dunno - don't have the words to describe it properly.
- neck and body 60secs
- head under shower (no rotation) 15secs
- start slow rotations as visualising cleanse/banish unwanted 'stuff'
- release feeling kicked in after three rotations

I began doing the rotation as a way to introduce the body gently to the cold water - each 360 clockwise rotation taking about 7 seconds to complete - but it now occurs to me that it could almost be a slo-mo variation of the Judson exercise but arms close rather than outstretched to remain in water stream.
I have not focussed on a 'banishing/cleansing' in such a way before (i.e. in a cold shower) - the experience was intense.

JMG and Commentariat - anyone else had any interesting stuff while experimenting with the cold water business?

Any thoughts appreciated.

Thanks,
[Earthworm]

replies.title: Re: Cold water and slo-mo Judson?
replies.content:
Interesting. I always start with my head, so for me the part where I feel reluctance just before the cold water arrives is the back of my shoulders, maybe just because that's usually the last part to get wet.

Three weeks in? Yes, this sounds familiar. You're getting accustomed enough that your perceptions range wider than the inevitable "wow I'm really doing this" and "just keep going" one tends to start out with. But besides that, it sounds like you had an accumulation of something that took a couple dozen showers for the cold water to dissolve and weaken. The feeling of cleansing is expected, but it might not always be so intense. (Another milestone you may soon pass, if you haven't already, is the first time you realize you're standing under a cold shower but thinking about something else entirely, like your to-do list or a conversation from the day before.)

Since you can shower cold for 2-3 minutes, have you considered washing in the cold water instead of switching over later? You can combine physical cleansing with visualizing the energy cleansing. Especially during a thorough final rinse. (If you're using bath products that don't seem to rinse clean with cold water, you can switch to others that do.)

I naturally rotate slowly (also clockwise) during my showers, but I never thought about that being for reasons besides distributing the water (first to get wet all over, then to wash various parts as they're facing away from the direct stream, and finally several times to rinse). I'll have to try rotating with intention and see whether it feels any different.

comments.title: Old houses and their tales
comments.content:
Roughly a year ago we had bought an old pub and guesthouse in our neighborhood that we are currently renovating. The former owner and the last person to run the pub (on a minimal level) died a few years ago and her children didn't take too much care of the house. There was and still is a strong affection of her children for the mother but neither the mother nor the children seemed to take any care for the house which was in a pityable condition when we bought it.

There is one of the former owners grandchildren, who is in her 20ies, that seems to have a strong affection for both the deceased grandmother and the house. Although she never lived there and was - as far as I know - only rarely there, she was very upset when the house was sold although she had no interest or capability to take care for it on her own. A few days ago while I was working on the house, she suddenly stood in the backyard and asked if she could see the house and if I could show her around. She almost immediately burst into tears and did so several times while I showed her around. Her father, who joined us after a while said that he is worried for her, since she the house seems to have a powerful impact on her and she would also frequently dream of it.

So that's the story. When she approached me a few days ago and burst into tears I immediately had the thought "poor child, she's carrying a burden that's not her own" and in the evening I cast a geomantic chart asking "Has her mood in regard to the guesthouse any significance for the plans we have for the house?".

This is what I got as response:

1 - cauda draconis, 2 - populus, 3 - carcer, 4 - populus, 5 - amissio, 6 - laetitia, 7 - populus, 8 - albus, 9 - cauda draconis, 10 - carcer, 11 - albus, 12 - albus

I take it as a matter for the 7th house, which would mean the answer is yes. I take populus 7->2 as a sign that she is indeed afflicted by forces outside of her own and this hinders her letting go of the house (7->4) (or what it symbolizes for her). Translation by albus (12 - 8) could indicate that it might be possibly to mediate in a friendly but emotionally detached manner. Cauda draconis as our significator passing to the 9th could mean several things. As for "shopping for answers" - it might be that a part of me is convinced that the matter will not play an important role for our plans with the house but I am a little bit touched by the suffering of that girl and maybe curious about the tale that lies beneath it. Then it can mean that we should either leave that matter alone or that we could put an end to it which we could achieve by investing some thoughts and meditation into it (1->9).

My current take on things is that I'll be open to it should she approach us another time but I will certainly not be active in that matter on my own since it is none of my business. I am lending to the hypothesis that it is some very old family thing. The deceased woman that last lived there was the last and not so wealthy member of a formerly very wealthy family that owned the house for centuries. Now the house has finally left the family and in very poor shape, beyond the capacities of anyone of her descendants to fix it (they said they see it that way and I guess they are right).

I'd be grateful for you to comment on my interpretation and conclusions - and furthermore: Is there any occult lore regarding the passing of an old house from one family to another that you could relate or point to?

Thank's a lot!
Nachtgurke

replies.title: Re: Old houses and their tales

As for house transference, there's a lot of folk magic around that; I don't know what the traditions are in your area, but in American folk magic you want to sweep the place with a new broom, starting in the kitchen and ending at the front door, and then mop the floor in the same pattern using a new mop and water to which various magically effective substances are added -- a cup of vinegar, a quarter cup of salt, and a tablespoon of hot pepper is a good mix.

replies.title: Re: Apports
replies.content:
The Spiritualist movement has a very odd history. There was a period of a few decades in the late 19th century when some Spiritualist mediums accomplished quite a lot of physically impossible things in circumstances that make fraud very, very unlikely. Then that faded out, and the fraud came in as mediums scrambled to find some way to make the show go on.

This sort of thing happens now and again -- a new sect shows up and, for a while, performs genuine miracles. Then the miracles dry up, not all at once, but a little at a time. The force that performs apports doesn't seem to be something that human beings can control -- it shows up when it chooses, for a while, and then goes away.

Rod Dreher has an interesting blog post (https://www.theamericanconservative.com/three-worlds-of-evangelicalism-the-gods-return/) up discussing (among other things) Jonathan Cahn's book Return of the Gods. comments.title: Discursive meditation
comments.content:
I just been catching up on Paul Graham’s essays and one on writing caught my eye - http://www.paulgraham.com/words.html - it struck me as being quite pertinent to discursive meditation, which on reflection could be considered a form of the writing he is talking about it. He finished with these paragraphs:
“If writing down your ideas always makes them more precise and more complete, then no one who hasn't written about a topic has fully formed ideas about it. And someone who never writes has no fully formed ideas about anything nontrivial.

It feels to them as if they do, especially if they're not in the habit of critically examining their own thinking. Ideas can feel complete. It's only when you try to put them into words that you discover they're not. So if you never subject your ideas to that test, you'll not only never have fully formed ideas, but also never realize it.”

Replace every instance of writing in that with discursive meditation and see what you think.

replies.title: Re: Discursive meditation
replies.content: Discursive meditation started out in written form. The Stoics and Neoplatonists used to write little essays on topics as a spiritual practice, and that gradually evolved into discursive meditation as we know it. So, yes, you may be on to something...

comments.title: Ancestors and Headstones

Hi JMG,

Last year, I asked about the importance of marking the graves of ancestors. You kindly shared your opinion that yes, it's important. Thank you for encouraging me to get off the fence and do something about this!

OtterGirl

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content: They're both more than full time jobs. If you devote the time and effort you need to take and hold a position of temporal power, you won't have the spare time and effort you need to become or remain an operative mage, and vice versa.

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content: This isn't clicking for me; lots of powerful people have routines where they get up in the morning and do such-and-such before their day starts. What prevents you from getting up and doing the middle pillar exercise, some meditation, and divination if you are in a position of temporal power? Or does it have to do with the habits of mind you need to carry with you from that into your day?

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
You need to do a lot more than the basic practices if you want to be a serious operative mage. By the same argument, somebody could be in a position of temporal power and still be a professional athlete; after all, they can get up early and do some exercises!

The basic practices I've set out are quite adequate for those who intend to practice occultism in the course of their ordinary lives, and as I've noted, you can get very far spiritually that way. If you want to be an operative mage of any power, though, you have to devote the kind of time and energy that an athlete puts into athletics or a musician puts into music.

I'm not sure how to feel about the idea from this week's main post that Taoist longevity/immortality alchemical preparations are specifically meant to protect the practitioner by giving a ruler mercury poisoning.
